Morges

VDVaud

Create Account (Free) Or try a free trial story first

Story Ideas

Each story is rooted in real local history and landmarks

The tulip that remembered

During the Fête de la Tulipe, a child touches a golden tulip in the Parc de l'Indépendance and is transported back to 1971, when the very first bulbs were planted. They must help the gardeners arrange 100,000 flowers before dawn, or the festival that would one day draw 300,000 visitors and win a World Tulip Summit prize will never come to exist.

Create This Story

Since 1971, the Parc de l'Indépendance has hosted the Fête de la Tulipe. Today approximately 150,000 tulips, daffodils, and hyacinths of some 350 varieties bloom each spring, drawing nearly 300,000 visitors and earning comparisons to the Keukenhof in the Netherlands. In 2024, the festival won Best Public Festival at the World Tulip Summit in The Hague. The park was renamed Parc de l'Indépendance in 1898 during celebrations marking 100 years of Vaudois independence from Bernese rule.

The castle's four museums

Each of the four museums inside the Château de Morges holds a guardian: a tiny tin soldier from the figurine collection, a Burgundian War cannon that whispers, a floating gendarme's kepi, and a glowing knight's sword. A child must unite all four guardians to save the castle from a storm rolling across Lac Léman, discovering the centuries of history each protects.

Create This Story

The Château de Morges, built in 1286 by Louis I of Savoy in the 'carré savoyard' plan with four round corner towers, today houses four museums: the Musée militaire vaudois, the Musée suisse de la figurine historique (with 10,000 figurines in nearly 50 dioramas), the Musée de l'artillerie (covering 500 years of Swiss artillery), and the Musée de la gendarmerie vaudoise. The castle served as the Bernese bailiff's residence for 262 years after the 1536 conquest of the Pays de Vaud. It is a Swiss heritage site of national significance.

Paderewski's lost melody

A child visiting the Musée Paderewski discovers a half-finished musical score hidden behind a portrait. When they play the first notes on a piano, the ghost of Paderewski appears at Riond-Bosson and asks them to help complete the melody before the moon sets over Mont Blanc — a story of music, memory, and the unfinished dreams of a pianist who became a prime minister.

Create This Story

Ignacy Jan Paderewski (1860-1941), the virtuoso Polish pianist and statesman, made Morges his home for over four decades. In 1897, he rented the Villa Riond-Bosson, a 26-room estate overlooking Lac Léman and Mont Blanc. In 1919, he became Prime Minister of newly independent Poland, leading the delegation to the Paris Peace Conference at Versailles. Today the Musée Paderewski within the Château de Morges preserves his photos, letters, scores, and personal objects.

Major Davel's 600 soldiers

A child finds a tin figurine of Major Davel in the castle museum and it comes alive, telling the story of his march on Lausanne in 1723 with 600 troops. The child must decide: help Davel deliver his manifesto to the council, or warn him of the betrayal that awaits — knowing that his sacrifice will one day inspire an entire canton's independence.

Create This Story

Jean Daniel Abraham Davel (1670-1723), known as Major Davel, is the great patriotic hero of Canton de Vaud. On 31 March 1723, using the pretext of a military parade, Davel marched 600 troops into Lausanne and presented a manifesto of grievances to the municipal council. He was arrested the next day, tortured twice, and beheaded on 24 April 1723. He maintained throughout that his mission was divinely inspired by a figure he called 'La belle inconnue.' His sacrifice is commemorated every 24 April. His memorabilia are held in the Morges castle museum.

The narrow-gauge adventure

A child boards the little BAM train at Morges station, but instead of the modern 'Joran,' the original 1895 steam locomotive appears and carries them through time. Each stop — Apples, L'Isle, the army barracks at Bière — reveals a different era of the Vaudois countryside, from vineyard workers to soldiers to the quiet rhythm of village life that the railway connects.

Create This Story

The Bière-Apples-Morges (BAM) railway, a metre-gauge line, was first discussed on 26 September 1875 and opened on 1 July 1895. It connects Morges to the villages of Apples and Bière, with a branch to L'Isle (opened 12 September 1896). Originally steam-powered, it was electrified in 1943. Since 2017, the line is served by modern Stadler trainsets nicknamed 'Le Joran' after the cold north wind. The line also carries military traffic to Swiss army installations near Bière.

Audrey's garden

A child picking vegetables in a Tolochenaz garden finds a hidden gate that leads to La Paisible as it was in the 1970s. Audrey Hepburn is there, tending her roses and preparing for a trip to the Morges market. Together they walk the Grand-Rue, and the child learns that the greatest adventures are sometimes the simplest ones — a morning at the market, flowers in a basket, sunlight on the lake.

Create This Story

Audrey Hepburn (1929-1993) lived for thirty years at La Paisible ('The Place of Peace'), an 18th-century farmhouse in Tolochenaz, just 2 km from Morges. Hepburn and her first husband Mel Ferrer purchased the property in 1963. She tended her vegetable garden, visited the Morges market on the Grand-Rue, and raised her family. She died at La Paisible on 20 January 1993 and was buried in the Tolochenaz cemetery. The Morges tourist office offers a walking itinerary 'In the Footsteps of Audrey Hepburn.'

The lake dwellers of Grande-Cité

Swimming off the Morges shore, a child dives underwater and surfaces in 1031 BC, among the Bronze Age pile dwellings of Grande-Cité. They must help the lake dwellers cast a bronze axe and trade fine ceramics before the water rises and the village must be moved to higher ground — a plunge into three thousand years of lakeside life.

Create This Story

Several prehistoric pile-dwelling settlements have been found along the Morges lakefront. The largest, Grande-Cité, dates from the late Bronze Age, with wooden objects dendrochronologically dated to 1031 BC. The settlement Les Roseaux dates from the Early Bronze Age and is rich in bronze axe-edge strips and fine ceramics. Both sites are part of the UNESCO World Heritage serial property 'Prehistoric Pile Dwellings around the Alps.'

The ship named Morges

A child boards the CGN paddle steamer 'Morges' for a routine lake crossing, but the Belle Epoque vessel sails into a fog and emerges in different centuries at each port: medieval Morges under Louis of Savoy, revolutionary Morges in 1798, tulip-festival Morges in full spring bloom. The child must find their way back to the present before the last whistle blows.

Create This Story

The CGN (Compagnie Générale de Navigation sur le lac Léman), founded in 1873, serves the Morges landing stage with both modern vessels and historic Belle Époque paddle steamers. The CGN named one of its ships 'Morges.' Louis I of Savoy founded the town in 1286 on a flat stretch of lakefront. On 24 January 1798, the Vaudois Revolution threw off Bernese rule, briefly forming the République lémanique. The Parc de l'Indépendance was renamed in 1898 to celebrate 100 years of Vaudois freedom.
